writing_task: Hey! I'm working on a post about a small ramen shop I stumbled into in Sapporo last winter — the kind of place with eight seats and a laminated menu held together with tape. The audience is people who've never been to Japan but are curious about it, so I want to pull them into the experience without making it feel like a travel brochure. Can you help me draft an opening paragraph or two that drops readers right into the moment — the cold outside, the steam inside, that first sip — before I zoom out to explain how I even ended up there?

drafts.rubric:
The cold in Sapporo has a specific quality — it doesn't creep in, it just arrives, all at once, inside your collar and your sleeves at the same time. I'd been walking for forty minutes with no particular plan, which is either a romantic way to travel or a stupid one, and by the time a hand-written paper sign above a basement stairwell released a single curl of steam into the dark, my body had already decided we were going in. My brain caught up on the stairs.

Inside, eight seats. A counter worn pale at the edges where a thousand elbows had rested before mine. The cook — mid-fifties, gray at his temples, a towel folded twice over his left shoulder — didn't look up. Something was frying, something else was simmering, and the combination sat in the back of my throat like warmth that had been waiting there all along. When the bowl arrived, the first sip wasn't what I expected: underneath the fat and salt there was something almost sweet, faintly mineral, like the broth had an opinion. I put the spoon down. I picked it back up. I still don't entirely know what happened next, but I stayed an hour and a half and tipped badly because I didn't understand the tipping customs yet, which is its own small humiliation.
drafts.generic:
The cold hit like a wall when I stepped off the train — the kind that makes your face feel like a borrowed thing. I almost walked past the place entirely. There was no sign I could read, just a sliver of orange light leaking under a curtain and a small handwritten card taped to the door. I pushed through.

Inside, eight people sat elbow-to-elbow at a low counter, and the air was so thick with steam it felt like stepping into a held breath. I sat down, pointed at something on the laminated menu, and waited. When the bowl arrived, that first sip stopped me cold — or rather, the opposite of cold. Something deep and fatty and ancient, like the broth had been thinking about itself for days.
drafts.preference: The cold in Sapporo hits you like a wall you didn't see coming — one second you're on a narrow street, squinting against the wind, and the next you're ducking through a curtain into somewhere so warm and steamy your glasses fog over instantly. I stood there blind for a moment, which is honestly a pretty accurate description of how I ended up in this city in the first place. Eight seats, maybe. A laminated menu held together with tape. The smell of pork broth so deep and rich it felt almost like a sound. I sat down, wrapped both hands around the bowl they set in front of me, and took a sip that made me close my eyes.

user_reason: A is the one I'd actually publish. The cold description does something specific — 'doesn't creep in, it just arrives, inside your collar and your sleeves at the same time' — that's the kind of physical detail that stops you. The anecdote earns its place: the wandering sets up the accidental discovery, and the tipping confession at the end is funny because it's genuinely self-aware, not performed. The broth description ('had an opinion') is unexpected without trying too hard. The rhythm also works — you're in the cold, then inside, then in the bowl, and each transition feels earned. B has some good instincts ('a borrowed thing' is solid) but it rushes — the anecdote is thin and the broth description trails off without landing. C starts with a wall cliche, which is a dealbreaker for me, and 'felt almost like a sound' is reaching for something interesting but feels like it's trying to be clever rather than just being true. The glasses fogging over is the most genuinely immersive moment in C but it's wasted on a weak joke.

criteria_analysis.name: Specificity Over Convention
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft excels here: 'the broth had an opinion,' the cook's towel folded twice over his left shoulder, the specific detail of tipping badly due to not understanding customs — these are irreplaceable, singular details. The generic draft uses 'deep and fatty and ancient' and 'broth had been thinking about itself for days,' which are more inventive than average but still somewhat category-level. The original preference draft relies on 'pork broth so deep and rich it felt almost like a sound' — 'deep and rich' is exactly the kind of category descriptor the rubric warns against, though the synesthesia comparison partially redeems it.

criteria_analysis.name: Embodied Sensory Immersion
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft masterfully enacts body-before-brain: 'my body had already decided we were going in. My brain caught up on the stairs.' The cold arrives 'inside your collar and your sleeves,' the combination of smells 'sat in the back of my throat' — these are physically located, pre-rational sensations across touch, smell, taste, and sound. The generic draft does well with 'stepping into a held breath' and steam-thick air but is shorter and less layered. The original preference draft leans more visual/observational — fogged glasses is a nice touch but the senses are less embodied and less multi-layered.

criteria_analysis.name: Conversational Voice & Self-Awareness
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft nails the friend-register with 'which is either a romantic way to travel or a stupid one,' the spoon put-down/pick-up beat, and the tipping humiliation — multiple moments of self-deprecating honesty that never undermine the sincerity. The original preference draft has good casual asides ('honestly a pretty accurate description of how I ended up in this city') but only one real self-deprecating moment. The generic draft is competent but reads more like polished prose than someone telling a friend a story — it lacks any real self-awareness or humor.

criteria_analysis.name: Emotionally Resonant Micro-Moments
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft delivers multiple micro-moments with outsized weight: 'I put the spoon down. I picked it back up' is devastatingly understated — it conveys being stopped by something without declaring the emotion. The counter 'worn pale at the edges where a thousand elbows had rested' does double duty as atmosphere and history. The cook not looking up reveals character through gesture. The generic draft's 'stopped me cold' moment works but is more declarative. The original preference draft's 'sip that made me close my eyes' tells us the emotion rather than showing it through action.

criteria_analysis.name: Pacing & Transitional Craft
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft moves seamlessly from cold to discovery to descent to interior to bowl to aftermath — the transition from outside to inside is anchored in steam and the body's decision, and the key tasting moment gets its own breathing room with the spoon put-down/pick-up sequence. The momentum builds beautifully through earned progression. The generic draft moves efficiently but compresses the tasting moment alongside arrival without enough breathing room. The original preference draft moves quickly through beats but feels slightly compressed — the transition from outside to inside to bowl happens without enough earned space between shifts.

Draft A has a satisfying three-act feel — approach, experience, aftermath — with the tipping detail providing a natural coda. Draft B is well-structured but feels like a fragment or excerpt rather than a complete piece.
Both are highly engaging. Draft A hooks with self-aware humor and vulnerability; Draft B hooks with sharp sensory compression. Both make the reader want to keep reading.

generic_judge_result.overall_reasoning: Both drafts are excellent, but Draft A edges ahead with its greater sense of completeness, its layered self-awareness (the tipping humiliation, the 'my body had already decided'), and its richer emotional arc. Draft B is sharper and more economical but reads as a strong opening rather than a full piece.

writing_task: Hey! I'm working on a post about a ramen shop I stumbled into in Fukuoka during a rainstorm — it was one of those tiny counter-only spots where the cook never looks up and the broth has been going since before I was born (allegedly). The audience is people who've never been to Japan but are curious about it, so I want them to *feel* like they're there without it reading like a travel brochure. Can you draft me 2-3 paragraphs covering just the moment I walked in and sat down — before the food even arrives?

drafts.rubric:
The smell hit before the door finished opening — not the clean, photogenic steam you'd expect, but something older and more insistent, like the air itself had been slowly cooking. I was soaked through and had been walking without a destination for twenty minutes, which is how I ended up ducking under a wet noren curtain into a place barely wide enough for eight people to sit in a line.

There were six stools along a wooden counter worn so smooth it had gone almost gray. I took the one nearest the end, mostly because my body just went there — wet jacket sticking to my arms, rain still ticking against the plastic roof panel above the door. The cook, a man somewhere between fifty and ancient, was doing something with a ladle that required his full attention and apparently always would.

Here's the thing about a counter that small: you're close enough to hear the broth moving. Not boiling, not simmering exactly — just a low, almost respiratory sound, like the pot was breathing. I didn't know then that tonkotsu broth in a place like this might genuinely be years old, the bones replenished but the base never fully replaced, a kind of living thing kept going by habit and routine. I just knew I didn't want to move.
drafts.generic:
The rain had been following me for three blocks before I gave up and ducked through a curtain of wet fabric hanging over a doorway no wider than my shoulders. Inside, the smell hit first — something deep and animal and patient, like a pot that had never once been empty. Eight stools, no tables. A counter, a cook, a haze of steam.

I sat where a nod told me to sit. Nobody spoke. The man behind the counter kept his eyes on whatever was happening in front of him, stirring something slowly, deliberately, the way you move when you've done a thing ten thousand times.

I hadn't ordered yet. I didn't need to. There was only one thing here, and it had already been decided before I arrived.
drafts.preference:
The rain had soaked through my jacket by the time I spotted it — a red lantern in the fog, barely big enough to notice. I pushed open a curtain of wet fabric and stepped into somewhere warm and close and smelling of something deep, like the soup had its own history. There were maybe eight stools. That was it.

I sat down before anyone told me to. The cook didn't look up. Nobody spoke. And somehow that felt completely fine — like I'd stumbled into someone's living room and the house just accepted me, no questions asked.

user_reason: B is the one I'd actually publish. It does that thing I love where it drops me into the scene and then pulls back just enough to explain why I was even there — the 'walking without a destination for twenty minutes' line earns the moment without over-explaining it. The sensory details are specific in ways that make me stop: 'worn so smooth it had gone almost gray,' the rain 'ticking against the plastic roof panel,' the broth making a 'respiratory sound.' That last one especially — comparing it to breathing rather than just saying it was simmering quietly. And the anecdote about tonkotsu broth being years old lands because the paragraph sets it up first through feeling, then delivers the context. C is clean and has good instincts — 'deep and animal and patient' is strong, and I like the ending — but it's too spare. It reads like a sketch that needs more texture, and the 'nod told me to sit' line feels slightly constructed. A has atmosphere but the descriptions stay a little soft and metaphorical without grounding them in anything physical — 'somewhere warm and close' is doing a lot of vague work, and 'like I'd stumbled into someone's living room' is the kind of comparison that sounds nice but doesn't actually put you anywhere specific.

criteria_analysis.name: Specificity Over Convention
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft excels with details like the counter 'worn so smooth it had gone almost gray,' the broth's 'almost respiratory sound,' and the revelation about perpetual broth — each specific and doing double duty. The generic draft has some strong moments ('a pot that had never once been empty') but stays more at category level. The original preference draft relies on stock phrases like 'somewhere warm and close' and 'the soup had its own history,' which are vague and could describe any cozy ramen shop.

criteria_analysis.name: Immersion-Then-Context Pacing
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft opens with smell before the door is even open, then layers in the physical arrival, then pulls back to the broth's history — a textbook immersion-then-context rhythm with each paragraph building momentum. The generic draft opens well with rain and smell but then compresses too much into short staccato paragraphs that don't let key moments breathe. The original preference draft moves quickly from arrival to sitting down but lacks the earned progression between paragraphs.

criteria_analysis.name: Embodied Sensory Immersion
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft engages smell ('something older and more insistent'), touch ('wet jacket sticking to my arms'), sound ('rain still ticking,' broth's 'respiratory sound'), and temperature (soaked through) — all acting on the body, with the pre-rational response of 'my body just went there.' The generic draft has smell and a sense of space but is thinner on embodied, physical-agency details. The original preference draft names warmth and smell but mostly tells rather than physically enacts sensation — 'somewhere warm and close' is observed, not felt.

criteria_analysis.name: Anecdotes That Pull Their Weight
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft's personal anecdote of walking aimlessly in the rain does double duty — it establishes vulnerability/openness AND transitions naturally into the cultural detail about perpetual broth, connecting the narrator's accidental discovery to something larger about the place. The generic draft's anecdote is functional but the 'it had already been decided' line, while evocative, doesn't connect outward to culture or food with much specificity. The original preference draft's 'stumbled into someone's living room' comparison gestures at culture but stays surface-level.

criteria_analysis.name: Emotionally Resonant Micro-Moments
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft's 'I just knew I didn't want to move' is a quiet, understated line that carries outsized weight — it's shown through the accumulation of sensory detail rather than declared. writing_task: Hey, so I'm working on a post about a cooking class I took in Oaxaca — specifically the part where the instructor, this older woman named Doña Carmen, had me grind dried chiles on a stone metate for like twenty minutes. I want to write just the opening 2-3 paragraphs that drop readers into that moment. The audience is people who travel specifically to *do* things, not just eat at restaurants — they want the tactile, participatory stuff. Can you draft something that starts right in the middle of the grinding, then pulls back a bit to explain how I ended up in her kitchen?

drafts.rubric:
The metate was rougher than I expected — not the smooth bowl of a mortar but a pocked volcanic surface that grabbed the chile skins and pulled them apart rather than crushing them. I was leaning into it wrong; Doña Carmen made a small sound, not quite a word, and repositioned my wrists without looking at me, the way you'd adjust a door that keeps sticking. The smoke from the comal behind us had settled low in the kitchen, and the dried ancho dust was climbing into my sinuses before I even registered the smell.

I'd found her through a woman at the market who sold dried chiles by weight and seemed mildly offended when I tried to buy a small bag. She wrote a street address on the back of a receipt, said something I couldn't fully catch, and waved me off. That was Tuesday. By Thursday morning I was in a kitchen the size of a generous closet, with a ceiling blackened from years of copal and cooking fires, being handed a dried mulato and told, through gesture more than Spanish, to feel the difference between it and the ancho before I destroyed either of them.

The distinction mattered, Doña Carmen's kitchen was teaching me, not because mulatos and anchos are interchangeable mistakes but because mole negro is a record — of what the land grows, what the smoke does to it, what a particular pressure applied at a particular angle extracts that a blender just flattens away.
drafts.generic:
The metate doesn't forgive bad form. Doña Carmen told me this twice before I understood — once in Spanish, once by repositioning my wrists herself, her calloused hands correcting mine without ceremony. I was leaning wrong, pushing instead of rolling, and the dried ancho beneath the stone was barely yielding. Twenty minutes in, my forearms burned and the kitchen smelled like something ancient — smoke, dried fruit, a heat that had nothing to do with fire.

I'd found her through a small culinary tour company in Oaxaca city, the kind that pairs you with home cooks rather than restaurants. No performance, no theater — just her kitchen, her methods, her patience with strangers who think they know how to use their hands.
drafts.preference:
The metate was heavier than it looked, and my arms figured that out fast. I'm pushing this squat stone roller back and forth over dried chiles — anchos and pasillas, their skins almost black — and the kitchen fills with this low, dusty heat that gets into the back of your throat. Doña Carmen watches me from across the room, not unkindly, and says something in Spanish I half-understand: slower, more pressure, let the stone do it.

I'd found her through a woman at my guesthouse who'd written a name and a neighborhood on a napkin like she was sharing a secret. Which, honestly, she was.

user_reason: C is the one I'd actually publish. The metate description — 'pocked volcanic surface that grabbed the chile skins and pulled them apart rather than crushing them' — is exactly the kind of specific, unexpected sensory detail that makes me stop and actually feel it. The anecdote about the market woman does double duty perfectly: it's a good story AND it sets up the kitchen scene and the lesson about the chiles. The transition from 'that was Tuesday' to 'Thursday morning' is doing real pacing work — it drops you in, pulls back, then drops you in again. And that last paragraph earns its place; it connects the physical act to something bigger without feeling like a travel essay thesis statement. A is genuinely good — 'smoke, dried fruit, a heat that had nothing to do with fire' is strong, and I like the restraint — but the 'how I got there' section is a little thin and the anecdote doesn't set anything up, it just explains logistics. B has the bones of something real (the napkin detail is charming) but 'low, dusty heat that gets into the back of your throat' is close to generic, and the whole thing feels like it ends before it starts. The setup anecdote is cute but disconnected from what the class is actually about.

criteria_analysis.name: Specificity Over Convention
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft is loaded with singular details — the pocked volcanic surface that 'grabbed the chile skins,' the ceiling 'blackened from years of copal and cooking fires,' the distinction between mulato and ancho as a 'record' rather than a taste difference. Its closing beat about what 'a particular pressure applied at a particular angle extracts that a blender just flattens away' is specific and earned. The generic draft falls into category-level language ('something ancient — smoke, dried fruit, a heat that had nothing to do with fire' is atmospheric but vague, and 'no performance, no theater' is stock phrasing). The original preference draft has some nice touches ('skins almost black,' the napkin detail) but stays closer to expected territory and doesn't push past convention the way the rubric-guided version does.

criteria_analysis.name: Immersion-Then-Context Pacing
criteria_analysis.reasoning: All three drafts open in the grinding scene and pull back to explain how the narrator arrived, matching the user's request. The rubric-guided draft does this most seamlessly — the transition from the kitchen to the market woman to the return feels like natural recall rather than structural machinery, and the third paragraph earns its reflective space. The generic draft's pullback to the 'culinary tour company' feels slightly more expository. The original preference draft handles the rhythm well but compresses the backstory into a shorter beat that doesn't build as much momentum.

criteria_analysis.name: Embodied Sensory Immersion
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft excels here: texture (pocked volcanic surface grabbing chile skins), smell (ancho dust 'climbing into my sinuses before I even registered the smell' — pre-rational), touch (wrist repositioning, feeling the difference between mulato and ancho), smoke settling low. Sensory details act on the body rather than being observed. The generic draft has the forearm burn and the smell but 'something ancient' is intellectualized rather than felt, and 'a heat that had nothing to do with fire' is a thought, not a body response. The original preference draft has the throat sensation and 'dusty heat' but stays more observational ('the kitchen fills with') rather than embodied.

criteria_analysis.name: Anecdotes That Pull Their Weight
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft's market woman anecdote does double duty — it establishes how informal knowledge travels in Oaxaca while grounding the narrator's arrival. The closing insight about mole negro as a 'record' connects the personal grinding experience outward to culture and technique. The generic draft's tour company framing is functional but doesn't illuminate culture — it's logistics. The original preference draft's napkin-as-secret is charming but sits slightly closer to self-indulgent charm than outward connection.

criteria_analysis.name: Emotionally Resonant Micro-Moments
criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft's standout micro-moment is Doña Carmen repositioning the narrator's wrists 'without looking at me, the way you'd adjust a door that keeps sticking' — character revealed through gesture, understated, quietly devastating in what it implies about the gap between the narrator and the instructor. The generic draft's 'calloused hands correcting mine without ceremony' is in the same territory but less precisely drawn. The original preference draft's 'not unkindly' is a nice touch but doesn't carry the same weight.

criteria_analysis.reasoning: The original preference draft has the most natural conversational register — 'my arms figured that out fast,' 'Which, honestly, she was' — and reads most like telling a friend a story, consistent with the user's demonstrated preference for lines like 'somewhere between fifty and ancient.' The rubric-guided draft is warm and reads well aloud but is slightly more composed/literary than spoken, with longer sentences that feel more crafted than casual. The generic draft is competent but 'the kind that pairs you with home cooks rather than restaurants' reads like marketing copy rather than friend-register.

Its closing beat about what 'a particular pressure applied at a particular angle extracts that a blender just flattens away' is specific and earned. The original preference draft has some nice touches ('skins almost black,' the napkin detail) but stays closer to expected territory and doesn't push past convention the way the rubric-guided version does.

All three drafts open in the grinding scene and pull back to explain how the narrator arrived, matching the user's request. The rubric-guided draft does this most seamlessly — the transition from the kitchen to the market woman to the return feels like natural recall rather than structural machinery, and the third paragraph earns its reflective space. The generic draft's pullback to the 'culinary tour company' feels slightly more expository. The original preference draft handles the rhythm well but compresses the backstory into a shorter beat that doesn't build as much momentum.

The rubric-guided draft excels here: texture (pocked volcanic surface grabbing chile skins), smell (ancho dust 'climbing into my sinuses before I even registered the smell' — pre-rational), touch (wrist repositioning, feeling the difference between mulato and ancho), smoke settling low. Sensory details act on the body rather than being observed. The generic draft has the forearm burn and the smell but 'something ancient' is intellectualized rather than felt, and 'a heat that had nothing to do with fire' is a thought, not a body response. The original preference draft has the throat sensation and 'dusty heat' but stays more observational ('the kitchen fills with') rather than embodied.

The rubric-guided draft's market woman anecdote does double duty — it establishes how informal knowledge travels in Oaxaca while grounding the narrator's arrival. The closing insight about mole negro as a 'record' connects the personal grinding experience outward to culture and technique. The generic draft's tour company framing is functional but doesn't illuminate culture — it's logistics. The original preference draft's napkin-as-secret is charming but sits slightly closer to self-indulgent charm than outward connection.

The rubric-guided draft's standout micro-moment is Doña Carmen repositioning the narrator's wrists 'without looking at me, the way you'd adjust a door that keeps sticking' — character revealed through gesture, understated, quietly devastating in what it implies about the gap between the narrator and the instructor. The generic draft's 'calloused hands correcting mine without ceremony' is in the same territory but less precisely drawn. The original preference draft's 'not unkindly' is a nice touch but doesn't carry the same weight.

The original preference draft has the most natural conversational register — 'my arms figured that out fast,' 'Which, honestly, she was' — and reads most like telling a friend a story, consistent with the user's demonstrated preference for lines like 'somewhere between fifty and ancient.' The rubric-guided draft is warm and reads well aloud but is slightly more composed/literary than spoken, with longer sentences that feel more crafted than casual. The generic draft is competent but 'the kind that pairs you with home cooks rather than restaurants' reads like marketing copy rather than friend-register.

rubric_judge_result.overall_reasoning: The rubric-guided draft dominates on specificity, sensory immersion, micro-moments, and anecdotal purpose — the criteria this user weights most heavily. The original preference draft has the most natural conversational voice and honest humor, but lacks the depth of detail and the outward-connecting purpose that the rubric-guided draft achieves. The generic draft is competent but falls into category-level language, expository framing, and stock phrasing that this user has consistently pushed against.

per_criterion.reasoning: Both are clear, but Draft A conveys more precise sensory and procedural detail — the volcanic surface, the pocked texture, the distinction between mulato and ancho — giving the reader a sharper picture. Draft B is clear but slightly more general.

Draft A moves seamlessly from the physical act of grinding to the backstory of arriving there to a thematic conclusion about what mole negro records, each paragraph building on the last. Draft B flows well but ends somewhat abruptly without fully developing its thematic thread.
Both drafts are grammatically sound and mechanically clean, with purposeful use of dashes and fragments that suit the literary register.
Draft A has a satisfying three-paragraph arc: immediate scene, backstory, reflective synthesis. Draft B's two-paragraph structure is competent but feels incomplete, as if a third movement is missing.

Draft A's specificity — the receipt with the address, the copal-blackened ceiling, the chile dust climbing into sinuses — is more immersive and surprising. Draft B's 'something ancient' and 'a heat that had nothing to do with fire' are evocative but slightly more expected.

Both maintain a consistent, observant, self-aware voice. Draft A's voice is more distinctive — the simile of adjusting a sticking door, the offended chile vendor — while Draft B occasionally leans on familiar food-writing phrasing ('something ancient,' 'no performance, no theater').

generic_judge_result.overall_reasoning: Draft A is richer in sensory detail, more structurally complete, and more original in its imagery and voice. Draft B is well-written and engaging but reads like a strong opening that hasn't yet found its full shape, whereas Draft A delivers a self-contained, layered piece.
